agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined. This is an early-stage concept with significant ambiguity.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given context (time, geography, etc.) — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds constraining how far automated pricing can deviate — thresholds undefined" },
      { "term": "Real time", "definition": "Pricing adjustments happen continuously or near-instantaneously in response to demand changes, not via batch or scheduled recalculation" },
      { "term": "Base price", "definition": "Implied but not stated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made" },
      { "term": "Price adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery's price as a function of current demand level" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery price must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. A VP could change the pricing strategy (e.g., to flat pricing during promotions). It is a business policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand above the high-demand threshold, the computed price MUST be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery price must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Symmetric pricing policy to BR-1. Management could decide to hold prices flat during low demand instead. This is a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand below the low-demand threshold, the computed price MUST be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — not via scheduled batch recalculations",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational SLA/policy. The business could decide to batch-adjust every 5 minutes and still call it 'real time enough.' It's a latency preference, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand level changes, the price offered to the next customer reflects the updated demand within the system's defined real-time window"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The system must be fully automated — no manual approval required for individual price changes",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a policy decision. The business could add a manual approval step for extreme adjustments without corrupting the system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments are applied without human intervention in the standard flow"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds (both ceiling and floor)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If guardrails are absent or bypassed, the system could produce absurd prices (e.g., $0.00 or $10,000 for a local delivery). This is a state integrity constraint — a price outside sane bounds represents a corrupt system output. The CEO explicitly called this out as necessary.",
      "testable_assertion": "No price produced by DynaPricing may be below the defined minimum floor or above the defined maximum ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The final delivery price must always be greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical for a paid delivery service. This is a hard data integrity constraint — no policy change should allow a non-positive price.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level and any input, the computed delivery price is strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically correlated with demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the implied behavioral contract of demand-based pricing. However, management could introduce exceptions (e.g., loyalty overrides, promotional caps). It is a strong policy, not a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, price(D1) >= price(D2)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices should not be so high as to lose customers or so low as to be unprofitable",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic intent, not a hard constraint. The thresholds for 'competitive' and 'profitable' are undefined and policy-driven.",
      "testable_assertion": "NOT DIRECTLY TESTABLE without defined margin thresholds and competitive benchmarks — requires further specification"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to extreme levels never seen before (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrail ceiling must cap the price. System must not produce a price above the maximum bound regardless of demand signal magnitude." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero (no delivery requests at all)", "expected_behavior": "Price must still be positive and above the floor guardrail. Zero demand should not produce a zero or negative price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (e.g., toggling between high and low every few seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — potential for customer confusion if quoted price changes between viewing and confirming. Needs specification on price-lock or quote-validity window." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple geographic zones with independent demand levels", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — spec does not clarify whether demand is global, regional, or route-specific. Different scoping produces radically different pricing." },
    { "scenario": "System failure or inability to determine current demand", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — must specify fallback pricing behavior. Should it use last-known price, base price, or refuse to quote?" },
    { "scenario": "Price guardrail ceiling is set below the base price, or floor is set above the ceiling", "expected_behavior": "Configuration error — system must reject invalid guardrail configurations at setup time, not at pricing time." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during emergencies", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — dynamic surge pricing during declared emergencies may violate price-gouging statutes in some jurisdictions. Guardrails may need legal-event overrides." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is quoted a price, then demand changes before order is confirmed", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — spec does not address price-locking or quote expiry. Critical for customer trust." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — surge pricing increases revenue during peak demand; competitive low pricing during troughs may increase volume. Risk of customer churn if perceived as unfair. Guardrail misconfiguration could cause significant revenue loss or margin erosion.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with sub-second latency, and guardrail configuration management. Adds operational complexity and a new failure mode (pricing engine outage).",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility may cause frustration or gaming behavior (waiting for lower prices). Positive: lower prices during off-peak could attract price-sensitive customers.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk from price-gouging laws during emergencies. Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Technical risk from automation without human oversight. Financial risk from guardrail misconfiguration.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices; may feel exploited during high demand or benefit during low demand. Need transparent communication." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes demand-dependent and harder to forecast. Need new forecasting models." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals must be surfaced in real time. Courier supply/demand balance directly affects pricing." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Unclear if higher prices translate to higher courier pay. If not, courier retention risk during surge periods." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review price-gouging regulations per jurisdiction. Dynamic pricing in essential services is legally sensitive." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need policies for price disputes and refund eligibility." },
      { "group": "Product/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model transparently to maintain brand trust." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The spec is an informal CEO statement from an all-hands meeting — not a product requirements document. It contains high-level intent ('demand-based pricing', 'guardrails', 'competitive and profitable') but lacks every critical detail: demand measurement methodology, guardrail thresholds, pricing algorithm, geographic scope, price-locking semantics, fallback behavior, regulatory considerations, and courier compensation implications. The phrase 'probably have some guardrails' introduces uncertainty about whether guardrails are even confirmed. Multiple ambiguities and missing contexts make it impossible to define reliable acceptance criteria.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Prices go up when demand is high (BR-1)",
      "Prices go down when demand is low (BR-2)",
      "System is automated (BR-4)",
      "Final price must be positive (BR-6)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail specifics — 'probably' suggests not yet committed",
      "Definition of 'demand' — volume? capacity ratio? geographic scope?",
      "Definition of 'real time' — sub-second? minutes?",
      "Price-locking/quote validity semantics",
      "Fallback behavior during system failure",
      "Regulatory compliance approach",
      "Courier compensation implications",
      "What 'competitive' and 'profitable' mean quantitatively"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails WILL be implemented despite the CEO saying 'probably' — treating this as a safety-critical requirement",
      "A base/reference price exists from which adjustments are made",
      "Demand is measurable and available as a real-time signal",
      "Price must always be positive (basic financial integrity)",
      "The system serves a delivery/courier business context"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO said 'probably have some guardrails' — it is ambiguous whether guardrails are a firm requirement or aspirational. For a fully automated pricing system, guardrails are safety-critical. This must be confirmed as a hard requirement before implementation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue team", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory (recommended)", "Treat guardrails as optional/phase-2"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as mandatory from day one. An automated system without bounds is a financial and reputational risk that no business should accept."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' — is it delivery request volume per hour? Ratio of requests to available couriers? Per-zone or global? The entire pricing model depends on this definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product/Marketing", "Operations/Dispatch", "Finance/Revenue team"],
      "options": ["Request volume in a time window", "Request-to-courier capacity ratio", "Composite demand index"],
      "recommendation": "Use request-to-courier capacity ratio per geographic zone — this most faithfully represents supply/demand economics in courier logistics."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of guardrail thresholds (price floor, price ceiling), pricing algorithm, adjustment magnitude, or how aggressively prices should respond to demand changes.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance/Revenue team", "Customers", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Define fixed floor/ceiling (e.g., 0.5x-3x base price)", "Define percentage-based bounds", "Define jurisdiction-specific bounds"],
      "recommendation": "Start with conservative fixed multiplier bounds (e.g., 0.7x to 2.0x base price) and tune based on data. Jurisdiction-specific overrides needed for legal compliance."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of price-locking behavior. When a customer sees a quoted price, is that price guaranteed for a window? Or can it change before order confirmation? This is critical for customer trust and has legal implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Customer Support", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Lock price for N minutes after quote", "Price is live until order confirmed (caveat emptor)", "Lock price at checkout initiation"],
      "recommendation": "Lock the quoted price for a defined validity window (e.g., 5 minutes). Changing prices between quote and confirmation will destroy customer trust."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No fallback pricing strategy defined for when the demand signal or pricing engine is unavailable. A fully automated system with no fallback is a single point of failure for the entire delivery business.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations/Dispatch", "Finance/Revenue team"],
      "options": ["Fall back to base price", "Fall back to last-known dynamic price", "Reject orders until system recovers"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to base price. Never reject orders due to pricing engine failure — that turns a pricing outage into a business outage."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The relationship between dynamic pricing and courier compensation is unspecified. If customers pay more during surges but couriers earn the same, it creates a fairness issue and courier retention risk. If couriers earn more, it changes the margin model.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Couriers/Drivers", "Finance/Revenue team", "Operations/Dispatch"],
      "options": ["Surge revenue goes entirely to company margin", "Surge revenue is shared with couriers (incentivizes supply during peak)", "Courier pay is independent of dynamic pricing"],
      "recommendation": "Share a portion of surge revenue with couriers — this is standard in ride-hailing/delivery and incentivizes courier availability when demand is highest, which is the whole point of dynamic pricing."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
